children.text: The materials gathered in this project do not form a coherent archive. They circulate across institutional reports, policy documents, documentary footage, and testimonies that do not converge into a single account of the Panama Canal watershed.

Rather than producing clarity, these materials reveal how evidence is structured through mediation. Official statements, technical assessments, and media narratives do not simply describe territorial change; they actively participate in its definition.

Testimony appears within this field not as an alternative source of truth, but as a form of knowledge that is continually repositioned in relation to administrative and infrastructural frameworks. Its legibility depends on the conditions of recording, circulation, and institutional framing that determine what can be heard, cited, or rendered credible.
The result is not a unified narrative of the watershed, but a field of uneven visibility. What is documented, what is planned, and what is experienced do not align. They coexist without resolution.

Autoridad del Canal de Panamá (ACP). 2023. Integrated Water Resource Management Study: Water Project Alternative Milestone Meeting (AMM) Report. Panama Canal. https://pancanal.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/2023-May_Water-Project-AMM-Study-A-Final-Repo.
Molina, Daniel. 2025. “El espía arrepentido del Canal de Panamá”. Revista Anfibia.
Herrera, Guillermo Castro. 2002. “On Cattle and Ships: Culture, History and Sustainable Development in Panama.” Environment and History 7 (2): 201–17.
Lasso, Marixa. 2019. Erased: The Untold Story of the Panama Canal. N.p.: Harvard University Press.
Zien, Katherine A. 2017. Sovereign Acts: Performing Race, Space, and Belonging in Panama and the Canal Zone. N.p.: Rutgers University Press.
